title: The Clinical Efficacy of Mindfulness-Based Treatments for Alcohol and Drugs Use Disorders: A Meta-Analytic Review of Randomized and Nonrandomized Controlled Trials
headline: The Clinical Efficacy of Mindfulness-Based Treatments for Alcohol and Drugs Use Disorders: A Meta-Analytic Rev
summary: A meta-analysis / systematic review of mindfulness / meditation examined stress & anxiety. Large effect sizes were associated to levels of perceived craving, negative affectivity, and post-traumatic symptoms.
keyFindings: Large effect sizes were associated to levels of perceived craving, negative affectivity, and post-traumatic symptoms.
limitations: Heterogeneity indexes, moderators, bias of publication, and Orwin's fail-safe number were also estimated.
Pooled estimates combine heterogeneous studies; effect sizes vary by practice, population, and control condition.

The practice studied (mindfulness / meditation) is not the Miracle of Mind practice, so its findings don't transfer directly — though the shared directed-attention component may make some transfer plausible (see “Why this matters for the 7-minute practice” below).
simplifiedTitle: The Clinical Efficacy of Mindfulness-Based Treatments for Alcohol and Drugs Use Disorders: A Meta-Analytic Rev
aiSnippet: Meta-analysis / Systematic review, European addiction research (2018). Large effect sizes were associated to levels of perceived craving, negative affectivity, and post-traumatic symptoms.
whyItMatters: MoM shares directed attention (the focused-attention component) in a brief, guided format with the practice studied here. Because the benefit is linked to that mechanism rather than to any one method, it's reasonable to expect it may extend to MoM as well — though MoM hasn't been studied directly for this outcome.
